title: Strategic Communication for Executives
description: How Staff+ engineers communicate effectively with Directors, VPs, and C-level executives at FAANG. Covers writing for execs (one-pagers, TL;DRs), presenting complex technical topics to non-technical audiences, using the BLUF (Bottom Line Up Front) framework, telling compelling technical stories, and running effective design reviews with senior leaders.

description.value:
Executives make decisions in 30 seconds. They do not have the time or patience for context, background, or history before getting to the point. The BLUF (Bottom Line Up Front) framework is the single most important communication pattern at FAANG: state your recommendation in the first sentence, then provide supporting evidence. BLUF formula: 'We should [do X] because [Y will happen] and it will [Z impact].' One sentence. No preamble. No 'let me give you some background first.'

The SAP Trap: SAP training teaches engineers to lead with context. A typical SAP communication opens with 'As you know, our current system was implemented in 2015 using the standard SAP best practices...' and buries the recommendation on page 3. At FAANG, this approach gets you interrupted: 'What do you want me to decide?' The BLUF approach saves everyone time: 'We should migrate the legacy ECC system to S/4HANA by Q3 2027 to avoid the 2027 ECC end-of-support deadline and reduce our annual maintenance cost by 40%. Here is the analysis supporting this recommendation.'

Practice: Write every email, design doc, and presentation with the recommendation first. Force yourself to delete the first paragraph (the context) and start with the second paragraph (the ask). If the communication works without the first paragraph, you have successfully applied BLUF. If it doesn't make sense, your recommendation is not clear enough. The BLUF discipline trains you to think clearly about what you actually want before you start writing.

The FAANG one-pager is the default communication format for any decision that needs executive approval. A VP at Google or Director at Meta receives 50+ documents per week. The one-pager that gets read is the one that respects the reader's time: one page, scannable, decision-ready. Structure: (1) Recommendation — one sentence stating what you want and why. (2) TL;DR — three bullet points summarizing the key facts. (3) Problem/Context — two paragraphs max. (4) Options Considered — a comparison table with pros, cons, and cost for each option. (5) Recommendation Rationale — one paragraph explaining why the recommended option wins. (6) Risks — three to five bullet points with mitigation strategies. (7) Next Steps — what you need from the executive (approval, resources, decision).

The TL;DR Culture: FAANG executives expect the TL;DR at the very top of every document. Not 'Executive Summary' — TL;DR. It signals that you know they are busy. A strong TL;DR example: 'This document proposes migrating our legacy SAP PI middleware to SAP Integration Suite / Cloud Integration by Q2 2026. • $1.2M annual license savings from retiring PI hardware. • 85% reduction in integration failure rates. • 6-month migration with zero business downtime.' Three bullets. No jargon. Every word carries weight.

The Options Table: Executives love tables because they enable comparison at a glance. Your table should have columns: Option, Description, Cost (engineering months), Risk Level, Timeline, and Recommendation (Selected/Rejected). Keep the table to 3-4 options. The executive should be able to glance at the table and understand why Option B is selected over Option A and C. Never present only one option — that looks like you didn't do the analysis. Never present more than four options — that looks like you can't narrow down.

sections.title: Presenting Technical Topics to Non-Technical Audiences

description.value:
VP-level audiences do not need architecture details, deployment topology, or code snippets. They need answers to five specific questions: (1) What problem does this solve? (2) What is the business impact? (3) How much does it cost? (4) When will it be done? (5) What is the risk of not doing it? Every slide, every sentence, every data point must tie back to one of these five questions. If it doesn't, cut it.

The Ladder of Abstraction: When presenting to executives, climb the ladder of abstraction. Start with the concrete business problem (e.g., 'Our order-to-cash process takes 4 days instead of 2 hours because our legacy integration hub fails nightly'). Then propose the technical solution at the right abstraction level (e.g., 'We will replace the integration hub with SAP Integration Suite, which guarantees 99.99% uptime and sub-second message processing'). Only descend into technical details if the executive asks. Usually they won't — they trust you to handle the technical execution. What they need is confidence that you have a plan, understand the risks, and can deliver on time and budget.

Executive Presentation Prep: Before any executive meeting, write down the five answers to the five questions above. Then write your 'one-sentence ask' — the single sentence that describes what you need from the executive (approval, budget, headcount, decision). Practice delivering the one-sentence ask in under 30 seconds. If you can't state it in 30 seconds, you haven't thought clearly enough. Every slide in your deck should support that one-sentence ask. If a slide doesn't support it, delete it.

Executive Presentation Prep Template:

One-Sentence Ask (30-second version):
"I need approval to allocate 6 engineers for 3 months to migrate our legacy PI middleware to SAP Integration Suite, saving $1.2M/year and reducing integration failures by 85%."

Five Questions to Answer Before the Meeting:
1. What problem does this solve?
   -> Current PI middleware fails 12x/month, causing 4-hour order processing delays
2. What is the business impact?
   -> $1.2M/year in license savings + 85% fewer failed integrations + 50% faster order processing
3. How much does it cost?
   -> 6 engineers x 3 months = $360K one-time + $50K/month cloud infra (vs $150K/month current)
4. When will it be done?
   -> Phase 1 (POC): 6 weeks | Phase 2 (Migration): 6 weeks | Phase 3 (Cutover): 2 weeks
5. What is the risk of not doing it?
   -> PI reaches end-of-support Dec 2027. After that: $500K emergency migration + security exposure

Slides to Prepare (max 5):
1. Title + One-Sentence Ask
2. The Problem (business impact, not technical)
3. The Solution (1-2 sentences + high-level timeline)
4. Cost & ROI (table: investment vs savings)
5. Risks & Mitigation + Decision Needed

Every technical story needs four elements: Tension — a problem that genuinely matters to the business. Protagonist — your team or you as the engineer who solved it. Journey — how you navigated tradeoffs, failures, and discoveries. Resolution — measurable impact that proves the solution worked. Without tension, there is no story. Without a protagonist, there is no ownership. Without a journey, there is no engineering depth. Without resolution, there is no credibility.

Framing Your SAP Migration Story: SAP engineers have rich technical stories but often frame them as feature rollouts rather than engineering journeys. Reframe your SAP experience using the four elements. Example tension: 'Our global ERP system processed 50M transactions per day but took 4 hours to synchronize inventory across 15 instances. Every hour of delay cost $200K in lost sales due to overselling.' That is a tension that any FAANG executive understands. Example protagonist: 'My team of 5 engineers owned the integration layer.' Example journey: 'We evaluated event-driven architecture vs batch processing, chose Event Mesh after a 4-week POC, designed a conflict resolution strategy for concurrent writes, and migrated incrementally over 3 months with zero downtime.' Example resolution: 'Inventory sync dropped from 4 hours to 3 seconds. Lost sales reduced by 92%. Architecture adopted by 3 other business units.'

The FAANG Difference: At FAANG, technical storytelling is a career skill. Staff+ engineers are expected to present their work in company-wide forums, write tech talks that get hundreds of viewers, and represent their organization in cross-company conversations. The ability to make architecture compelling is what separates Staff+ engineers who are known across the company from those who are only known within their team. Practice telling your technical story in 60 seconds, 5 minutes, and 15 minutes. Each version should have the same four elements but different levels of detail.

value.question: Which communication format should you use based on your audience and decision type?

options.label: Executive needs to approve a budget or strategic decision
options.recommendation: One-pager (1 page). Structure: Recommendation (1 sentence), TL;DR (3 bullets), Problem/Context (2 paragraphs), Options Table (3-4 rows), Recommendation Rationale (1 paragraph), Risks (3-5 bullets), Next Steps. Circulate 48h before the meeting.

options.label: VP or Director wants a live presentation with discussion
options.recommendation: Slide deck (5 slides max). Slide 1: Title + One-Sentence Ask. Slide 2: The Problem (business impact). Slide 3: The Solution (high-level architecture + timeline). Slide 4: Cost & ROI. Slide 5: Risks + Decision Needed. No more than 3 bullet points per slide.

options.label: Peer engineers need to review and critique a technical decision
options.recommendation: Design doc (3-5 pages). Sections: Problem Statement, Context & Background, Proposed Design with C4 diagrams, Alternatives Considered with tradeoff table, Security & Privacy Review, Open Questions. Circulate to reviewers 48h before the review meeting.

options.label: Team needs a decision documented for future reference
options.recommendation: Async doc / lightweight memo (1-2 pages). Sections: Decision, Context, Rationale, Options Considered, Consequences. No meeting needed. Share in Slack/email with a request for comments within 48h. Good for low-risk decisions where team is distributed.

sections.title: Running Design Reviews with Senior Leaders

description.value:
Design reviews with Directors and VPs follow a different cadence than peer design reviews. The senior leader is not there to debate technical minutiae — they are there to make a decision, assess risk, and ensure alignment with organizational strategy. Your job is to make that decision easy. Before the review: Circulate the design doc 48 hours in advance. No exceptions. If the doc is not read, the review is wasted. Include a 'Decision Needed' callout box at the very top of the doc so the executive knows exactly what you need from them before they start reading.

During the review: State the decision needed in the first 30 seconds. Then present your recommendation (10 seconds). Then briefly walk through the options considered, focusing on why the rejected options were rejected (2 minutes). Then open for feedback: 'Here is where I need your input specifically — our timeline assumes 6 engineers but we only have 4 available. Should we hire 2 contractors or extend the timeline by 2 months?' This framing gives the executive a concrete decision to make rather than an open-ended discussion. After the review: Send a decision summary within 24 hours. Include the decision made, action items with owners, and next steps. This closes the loop and creates a written record. If no decision was reached, state what is blocking the decision and propose a follow-up timeline.

The Reinforcements Pattern: If you anticipate pushback on your recommendation, bring allies. Before a high-stakes design review, 1:1 with key stakeholders (your manager, the tech lead of the dependent team, the product manager). Get their buy-in before the meeting. In the design review, they become your reinforcement: 'I reviewed the proposal and I agree with the recommendation because our team depends on this migration being completed by Q2.' This pattern is standard at FAANG and is not considered politicking — it is considered preparation. The worst thing that can happen in a VP-level design review is a surprise objection that could have been resolved beforehand.

details: Staff+ Insight: The single most important communication skill for FAANG Staff+ is the ability to write a one-page memo that a VP reads in 2 minutes and makes a decision on. SAP engineers tend to over-communicate context before making the recommendation. At FAANG, lead with the recommendation. 'We should migrate to Event Mesh because it will reduce integration failures by 85% and save $1.2M/year. Here's why...' — that opening establishes clarity. Everything after that is supporting evidence. Practice writing every communication with the recommendation first, context second.
